frameworks.name: top-down control of visual attention
frameworks.one_line: cognitive-psychology models of voluntary attentional selection in primates

frameworks.name: transformer attention
frameworks.one_line: ML inductive bias inspired by but formally distinct from biological attention

frameworks.name: saliency-based bottom-up attention
frameworks.one_line: stimulus-driven competitive selection cited as a parallel construct

core_question: How do conceptual notions of attention in primate vision, cognitive psychology, and transformer-based machine learning align and diverge?
key_assumptions: attention is the flexible control of limited computational resources
biological and ML formalisms share enough structure to be compared
top-down control and saliency are useful organizing constructs
manipulations: review-level contrast across primate vision, cognitive psychology, and transformer ML
key_findings: attention has been studied alongside awareness, vigilance, saliency, executive control, and learning
attention has been recently adopted across several machine-learning domains
biological-attention research and ML attention have a complicated conceptual relationship
interpretations: biological and ML attention formalisms partially align but also conceptually diverge
the transformer attention mechanism is best read as an inductive bias loosely inspired by primate top-down control
